blurb: Fringed, almost spidery blue cornflowers on silver-felted stems in late spring — beautiful and vigorous, but it spreads by stolons and seeds so manage it with deadheading and dividing.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
spread: Spreads by stolons and self-sows; can be aggressive — divide and deadhead to contain
seasons: Blue-violet fringed cornflowers May–Jul (reblooms with deadheading); silver-woolly foliage; dies back in winter
wildlife: Good pollinator plant — bees, bumblebees, and butterflies, especially during the late-spring gap before many other perennials peak
deer: Generally deer-resistant due to woolly foliage
toxic: No serious toxicity reported, but Centaurea montana is regarded as inedible (medicinal use only) — not the same as the edible annual cornflower
origin: Mountains of central and southern Europe
habitat: Mountain meadows, open rocky slopes, and forest edges

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–8; fully cold-hardy on the Front Range (5b–6a). Dies back to the ground in winter with no protection needed. The crown and surface stolons are very cold-tolerant. Note: this is Centaurea montana, a European garden perennial — do NOT confuse with the noxious knapweeds (C. diffusa, C. stoebe, C. solstitialis) that are CO-listed weeds. Mountain bluet is not on any Colorado noxious weed list, but it is aggressive in some settings and should be managed.[1]
care.planting: Plant in-ground in spring (late April–May) or fall (September). Spring is preferred on the Front Range — early establishment lets the root system get anchored before the plant's natural tendency to spread kicks in. In containers, plant in spring; mountain bluet is vigorous and will fill and potentially crowd a pot within a season or two — use a substantial container (12 in+ diameter) and expect to repot or divide annually. Container roots are less cold-hardy; move pots to an unheated garage or sink them into the ground over winter rather than leaving them on an exposed surface.[1]
care.sun: Prefers full sun (6+ hours) for the strongest bloom and most compact habit. Tolerates light to moderate shade (part sun, 3–4 hours), especially in afternoon, which can extend flowering in summer heat; but in shade the plant stretches and is more prone to flopping. Front Range high-altitude sun is fine.[1]
care.soil: Adaptable — grows in average to poor, well-drained soil. Tolerates Front Range alkaline clay-loam; improves with modest amendment (compost) but does not need rich soil. Good drainage is important, especially in winter wet. It is not particular about pH within a reasonable range (6.0–7.5). On lean, gritty soil it is less aggressive and easier to contain.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate — water regularly while establishing (first season), then deeply every 1–2 weeks in summer. Once established it handles dry spells well. Avoid constantly wet soil, which encourages rot and exuberant spreading.[1]
care.spacing: Space 18 in apart. Plants spread by surface stolons and can expand 12–18 in per year in good soil, so allow room and plan to divide regularly. Planting near a physical barrier (path edge, shallow edging) can limit lateral spread.[1]
care.propagation: Division is the easiest and most reliable method: lift and divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring (March–April) or fall; replant vigorous outer sections and discard the congested center. This is also the primary way to contain spreading. Seed germinates readily without pre-treatment — sow indoors at room temperature 6–8 weeks before last frost, or direct-sow in fall for spring germination. Named or white-flowered selections may not come true from seed.[1]
care.bloom: Late spring into summer on the Front Range — typically May through July, with deadheading extending bloom into late summer or fall. The main flush of fringed blue-violet cornflowers is vigorous; prompt deadheading importantly prevents self-sowing as well as extending the display. A hard cutback after the first main flush often induces a lighter rebloom in late summer or fall.[1]
care.feeding: Light feeder — a single balanced slow-release application in spring is sufficient, or none at all on average soil. Rich soil and high nitrogen make it more aggressive and floppier; lean soil is better.[1]
care.maintenance: Deadhead diligently as flowers fade — this both extends bloom and, critically, prevents self-seeding onto neighboring plants or into natural areas. Cut plants back by half after the main June flush to encourage rebloom and keep the clump compact. Divide every 2–3 years in spring to control spread and maintain vigor. Remove any seedlings that appear where they are not wanted.[1]
care.selfsow: Self-sows readily if spent flowers are not removed before seed sets — volunteers appear around and beyond the parent clump. Combined with stolon spread, an unmanaged plant can colonize significant ground within a few seasons. Deadhead before seed is ripe and divide the plant annually to keep it in bounds.[1]
care.troubles: Not a CO-listed noxious weed, but mountain bluet is genuinely aggressive — stolon spread plus self-seeding can make it weedy in borders or near naturalized areas. Manage by deadheading and dividing; do not plant it where it can escape into open ground. Powdery mildew sometimes appears in hot, stagnant conditions in late summer; improve airflow and cut the plant back if affected. Occasional aphids; otherwise pest-free. Do not confuse with the true noxious knapweeds (diffuse, spotted, and yellow starthistle) — mountain bluet has larger, solitary blue flowers and silvery-woolly stems; the knapweeds have smaller pink-purple or yellow flowers and are invasive annuals/biennials.[1]
care.companions: Pairs well with other late-spring and early-summer perennials that can hold their own against a spreader: salvia, geranium 'Rozanne', catmint, and ornamental grasses. Avoid planting beside small or slow-growing plants that could be overwhelmed.[1]

edible.summary: Mountain bluet (Centaurea montana) is regarded as inedible — unlike its edible relative the annual cornflower (C. cyanus), it is not a food plant, though it has folk-medicinal use.[2]
edible.caution: Do not assume mountain bluet's flowers are edible just because the annual cornflower (Centaurea cyanus) is used as an edible garnish — they are different species. Centaurea montana is described as inedible (used as a medicinal plant in Central Europe, not as a food). No part is documented as a safe edible, and no specific toxic compound or severe poisoning is reported; treat it as ornamental only and do not eat it. (This is the harmless garden perennial, not one of the CO-listed noxious knapweeds.)[2]
